coredata.dc:title: Electrochemical detection of fipronil in vegetable and fruit based on Ti3C2TX-Chinese tower-like nanogold, DNA tetrahedron and catalytic hairpin assembly with outstanding sensitivity, selectivity and reproducibility
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Ruiyi, Li
Mengyu, Wei
Xiulan, Sun
Zaijun, Li
Xiaohao, Liu
coredata.dc:description: The current electrochemical methods cannot meet the requirements of sensitivity, specificity and stability for detection of pesticides in vegetable and fruit. The paper reports an electrochemical method for detection of fipronil integrating Ti3C2TX-Chinese tower-like nanogold (CT-Au) with DNA tetrahedron (T-DNA) and catalytic hairpin assembly. Firstly, CT-Au was synthesized by formation, growth of trioctahedron gold nanocrystal and subsequent structural evolution in L-glutathione. Then, it was combined with Ti3C2TX to form Ti3C2TX-CT-Au hydrogel via self-assembly of Ti3C2TX nanosheets induced by arginine-functionalized graphene quantum dot. The resulting Ti3C2TX-CT-Au film offers good catalytic activity owing to more exposure of high-index faces, conductivity and mechanical property owing to the existence of three-dimensional Ti3C2TX network. The modified electrode with Ti3C2TX-CT-Au was developed for electrochemical detection of fipronil, coupling with T-DNA and DNA cycle. In the presence of fipronil, fipronil triggers the DNA cycle and results in the immobilization of ferrocene molecules on the electrode surface, producing a significant signal amplification. Differential pulse voltammetry current linearly increases with increasing fipronil between 1 × 10−18 and 1 × 10−13 M with detection limit of 4.3 × 10−19 M (S/N = 3). The proposed analytical method exhibits outstanding sensitivity, selectivity and reproducibility. It has been satisfactorily applied in electrochemical detection of fipronil in vegetable and fruit.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Electrochemical sensor
Fipronil
Gold nanoparticle
Ti3C2TX
Catalytic hairpin assembly

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Optical micrograph (A) and AFM image (B) of the Ti3C2TX-CT-Au hydrogel with A-GQD+, η-λ curves (C) of the Ti3C2TX-CT-Au hydrogel without (a) and with A-GQD+ (b), AFM images of the Ti3C2TX film (D) and Ti3C2TX-CT-Au film (E), and stress–strain curves (F) of the Ti3C2TX film (a) and Ti3C2TX-CT-Au film (b).
SEM image of CT-Au (A), and SEM (B), TEM (C), HRTEM images (D) and element mappings of C (E), Ti (F), Au (G), S (H) and Cl (I) of Ti3C2TX-CT-Au film.
Total XPS spectrum (A), high-resolution spectra of Au4f (B), C1s (C), N1s (D) and Ti2p (E), and XRD patterns (G) of Ti3C2TX-CT-Au and XRD patterns (H) of CT-Au and Au.

The construction procedure of modified electrode (A), electrophoregram (B) of the reaction pathways of DNA cycle amplification: D-1 (lane 1), D-2 (lane 2), D-3 (lane 3), D-4 (lane 4), D-1 + D-2 + D-3 + D-4 (lane 5), H1 (lane 6), H1 + fipronil (lane 7), H2 (lane 8), H1 + H2 + T-DNA + 1 × 10-13 M fipronil (lane 9) and H1 + H2 + T-DNA (lane 10), CV curves in 1.0 mM K4Fe(CN)6 in the PBS of pH 7.4 at the scan rate of 10 mV s−1 (C) and Nyquist plots (Insert: equivalent circuit) in 1.0 mM K4Fe(CN)6 in the PBS of pH 7.4 (D) of bare GCE (a), Ti3C2TX-CT-Au/GCE (b), T-DNA/Ti3C2TX-CT-Au/GCE (c), MCH/T-DNA/Ti3C2TX-CT-Au/GCE (d), H1/fipronil/MCH/T-DNA/Ti3C2TX-CT-Au/GCE (e) and H2-H1/MCH/T-DNA/Ti3C2TX-CT-Au/GCE, and CV curves in 1.0 mM K4Fe(CN)6 in the PBS of pH 7.4 (E) of the as-proposed modified electrode at the scan rates of 20, 40, 60, 80, 100, 120, 140, 160, 180 and 200 mV s−1.

DPV curves in the absence and the presence of 1 × 10−13 M fipronil (A), relationship curves of DPV peak current caused by 1 × 10−13 M fipronil with T-DNA concentration (B), T-DNA incubation time (C), H2 incubation time (D) and H2 concentration (E), DPV peak currents for different modified electrodes, DPV curves in the presence of fipronil concentration of 0.0, 1 × 10−18, 5 × 10−17, 1 × 10−17, 5 × 10−17, 1 × 10−16, 5 × 10−16, 1 × 10−15, 5 × 10−15, 1 × 10−14, 5 × 10−14, 1 × 10−13 (from bottom to top), relationship curve of DPV peak current with logarithmic value of fipronil concentration, and DPV peak current values caused by different species, in which the specie concentration is 1 × 10−13 M. A PBS of pH 7.4 was employed as the electrolyte for the above all DPV detections.
Comparison of the analytical behaviors of different electrochemical methods for detection of fipronil.
The results for detection of fipronil in vegetable and fruit by proposed method and HPLC-MS.

head.ce:title: Electrochemical detection of fipronil in vegetable and fruit based on Ti3C2TX-Chinese tower-like nanogold, DNA tetrahedron and catalytic hairpin assembly with outstanding sensitivity, selectivity and reproducibility

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Synthetic and Biological Colloids, Ministry of Education, School of Chemical and Material Engineering, School of Life Sciences and Health Engineering, and School of Food Science and Technology, Jiangnan University, Wuxi 214122, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Synthetic and Biological Colloids
School of Chemical and Material Engineering
School of Life Sciences and Health Engineering, and School of Food Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Synthetic and Biological Colloids, Ministry of Education, School of Chemical and Material Engineering, School of Life Sciences and Health Engineering, and School of Food Science and Technology, Jiangnan University, Wuxi 214122, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The current electrochemical methods cannot meet the requirements of sensitivity, specificity and stability for detection of pesticides in vegetable and fruit. The paper reports an electrochemical method for detection of fipronil integrating Ti3C2TX-Chinese tower-like nanogold (CT-Au) with DNA tetrahedron (T-DNA) and catalytic hairpin assembly. Firstly, CT-Au was synthesized by formation, growth of trioctahedron gold nanocrystal and subsequent structural evolution in L-glutathione. Then, it was combined with Ti3C2TX to form Ti3C2TX-CT-Au hydrogel via self-assembly of Ti3C2TX nanosheets induced by arginine-functionalized graphene quantum dot. The resulting Ti3C2TX-CT-Au film offers good catalytic activity owing to more exposure of high-index faces, conductivity and mechanical property owing to the existence of three-dimensional Ti3C2TX network. The modified electrode with Ti3C2TX-CT-Au was developed for electrochemical detection of fipronil, coupling with T-DNA and DNA cycle. In the presence of fipronil, fipronil triggers the DNA cycle and results in the immobilization of ferrocene molecules on the electrode surface, producing a significant signal amplification. Differential pulse voltammetry current linearly increases with increasing fipronil between 1 × 10−18 and 1 × 10−13 M with detection limit of 4.3 × 10−19 M (S/N = 3). The proposed analytical method exhibits outstanding sensitivity, selectivity and reproducibility. It has been satisfactorily applied in electrochemical detection of fipronil in vegetable and fruit.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Fipronil is an efficient pesticide in agricultural production to treat seed, control sanitary pest and in poultry farming to kill insects [1]. Due to high toxicity and ecological risk, many countries have banned on the use of fipronil in agricultural crops [2]. However, fipronil is still found in food because of its high insecticidal efficiency. Development of a reliable technique for detection of fipronil is crucial to ensure human health. The current analytical methods for fipronil mainly include competitive enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) [3], immunosensor [4], fluorescent method [5], surface-enhanced Raman scattering (SERS) [6], gas chromatography (GC) [7], mid-infrared spectroscopy (MIR) [8], high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [9], gas chromatography-massspectrometry (GC–MS) [10], HPLC-MS [11] and electrochemicalsensor [12]. ELISA and immunosensor can offer high sensitivity and selectivity, but these need the use of special enzyme or antibody. However, environmental factors such as temperature always influences on the activity of enzyme and antibody. This may produce an obvious change in the detection signal and leads to a poor repeatability. Fluorescent method and SERS are highly sensitive, but the instability in detection signal restricts their actual uses. MIR is non-contact method that is more suitable for rapid screening, but it is susceptible to the matrix interference from biological samples. HPLC-MS indicates high sensitivity and selectivity, but it needs the use of professional operators, expensive instruments and strict sample pre-treatment. Electrochemical sensor causes a more interest in detection of fipronil because of rapid response, ease of operation, high sensitivity and good selectivity [13].

Some nanomaterials have been developed for construction of electrochemical sensor such as titanium dioxide [13], silver [5], black phosphorus nanosheet [14], gold [15], bismuth ferrite [16], ferric oxide [17] and zinc oxide [18]. Titanium dioxide, bismuth ferrite, ferric oxide and zinc oxide as n-type semiconductor are of good catalytic ability, but extremely low electronic conductivity leads to a poor sensitivity. To resolve the problem, some conductive two-dimensional carbon materials such as Ti3C2TX were introduced to make metal oxide-based nanocomposite [19]. Nevertheless, a great energy barrier between the two-dimensional material and the metal oxide crystal prevents high-speed electron transfer at the interface. Different from metal oxides and black phosphorus nanosheet, noble metal nanomaterial can offer good catalytic activity and electronic conductivity. Among noble metal nanomaterials, gold is a more ideal sensing material owing to its better catalytic activity and ease of fixation of biomolecules on the electrode by Au-S bond [20,21]. However, the current gold nanomaterials still exist three shortcomings as the electrochemical sensing material. One is the high-index faces of gold nanocrystal were covered by the low-index faces during the synthesis. This leads to a reduced catalytic activity. Another is gold nanocrystal lacks one stable there-dimensional structure. This hinders fast electrolyte transport and leads to a reduced sensitivity. In addition, the immobilization of gold nanomaterial on the electrode surface needs the use of non-conductive adhesive. This brings a significant decrease in the electronic conductivity of modified layer. To date, construction of a gold sensing material with ideal catalytic activity, conductivity and mechanical property is still highly anticipated.

In the study, we report an electrochemical method for detection of fipronil integrating Ti3C2TX-Chinese tower-like nanogold (Ti3C2TX-CT-Au), DNA tetrahedron (T-DNA) and catalytic hairpin assembly. The introduction of L-glutathione improves the exposure of high-index facets of gold nanocrystal. The introduction of protonated arginine-functionalized graphene quantum dot (A-GQD+) induces a rapid self-assembly of Ti3C2TX nanosheets and leads to the formation of monolithic Ti3C2TX network. The combination of CT-Au with monolithic Ti3C2TX network achieves to good catalytic activity, electronic conductivity and mechanical property. The proposed analytical method based on Ti3C2TX-CT-Au, T-DNA and catalytic hairpin assembly exhibits outstanding sensitivity, selectivity and reproducibility for detection of fipronil.

DNA sequence 1 (D-1, 5′-ACATTCCTAAGTCTGAAACATTACAGCTTGCTACACGAGAAGA GCCGCCATAGTATTTCGTTCCACACACTGAAAGACGAGTGTTACCCGTTTCAGTGTGG-3′, DNA sequence 2 (D-2, HO–(CH2)6-S-S-C6-5′-TATCACCAGGCAGTTGACAGTGTAGCAA GCTGTAATAGATGCGAGGGTCCAATAC-3′), DNA sequence 3 (D-3, HO–(CH2)6-S-S-C6-5′-TCAACTGCCTGGTGATAAAACGACACTACGTGGGAATCTACTATGGCGGCTCTTC-3′), DNA sequence 4 (D-4, HO–(CH2)6-S-S-C6-5′-TTCAGACTTAGGAATGTGCTTCCCACGTAGT GTCGTTTGTATTGGACCCTCGCA-T-3′), hairpin DNA 1 (H1, 5′-TGTACCGTCTGAGCGA TTCGTACAGTTTCTGGAGGACTGGGCGGGGTGACGGTTATGAGCAGTCAGTGTTAAGGAGTGCGGGGTAACACTGACTGC-3′) and hairpin DNA 2 (H2, 5′-CCACACTGAAACGGGT AACACT CGTCTTTCAGTGTGTCCTTGC-Fc-3′) were purchased from Sangon Biotech. Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). All DNA sequences were dispersed in the buffer solution of pH 7.4 containing 20 mM Tris-HCl, 1 mM MgCl2, 1 mM CaCl2 and 5 mM KCI and stored at −20 ℃ before use. H1 and H2 were diluted to 0.5 μM by the buffer solution of pH 7.4, containing 20 mM Tris-HCl, 1 mM MgCl2, 1 mM CaCl2 and 5 mM KCI. To form the hairpin structure, H1 and H2 were heated at 95 °C for 5 min and subsequently cooled to room temperature.

Scanning electron microscope (SEM) was carried out in HITACHI S4800 field emission scanning electron microscope. X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern was measured on the X-ray D8 Advance Instrument operated at 40 kV and 20 mA and using a Cu Kα radiation source with λ = 0.15406 nm. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopic (XPS) measurements were performed on the ESCALAB 250Xi photoemission spectrometer having Al Kα (1486.6 eV) dual anode as the source operating at 14.6 kV of anode voltage and 13.5 mA filament current. The XPS data was collected with pass energy of 20 eV at 4xl0-9 m bar vacuum. Initially the XPS unit was calibrated using Fermi edge of Ag (KE 1482.544). The C 1 s line of adventitious carbon with BE = 284.8 eV was taken as a reference for surface-charging corrections. After subtracting the linear-type background (or Shirley background for fitting the full Ta 4f regions), the core-level spectra were decomposed into their components with mixed Gaussian-Lorentzian lines by a non-linear least squares curve-fitting procedure, using the CasaXPS software. The binding energies and FWHM of the peaks were determined from the fitting results. The polyacrylamide gel electrophoresis (PAGE) was imaged with BIO-RAD ChemiDoc XRs.

CT-Au was synthesized by our previous method [22] unless the replace of D/L-cysteine by L-glutathione. The resulted CT-Au stock solution (4 mL, 5 mg mL−1) was mixed with 10 mL of 20 mg mL−1 Ti3C2TX solution. Then, dropped 5 mL of 3 mg mL−1 A-GQD+ solution and continued stirring for 1 h to form Ti3C2TX-CT-Au hydrogel.

D-1, D-2, D-3 and D-4 were diluted to 4 μM by the buffer solution of pH 7.4, composing of 20 mM Tris-HCl, 5 mM KCI, 1 mM CaCl2 and 1 mM MgCl2. Then, equal molar D-1, D-2, D-3 and D-4 were mixed, heated it at 95 °C for 5 min and cooled to room temperature. The formed T-DNA was stored at −20 ℃ before use.

Dropped 5 μL of the prepared Ti3C2TX-CT-Au hydrogel onto glass carbon electrode (GCE, 1 mm in diameter) and subsequently dried at 50 °C in vacuum to form Ti3C2TX-CT-Au film. Then, 1 μM of T-DNA solution was mixed with an equal volume of 1 mM TCEP and incubated for 1 h in air bath shaker. Its 10 μL was dropped onto the surface of Ti3C2TX-CT-Au/GCE, incubated at 37 °C for 2 h, washed with ultrapure water and blow-dried with N2 in sequence. Finally, dropped 10 μL of 1 mM MCH solution onto the surface of T-DNA/Ti3C2TX-CT-Au/GCE. The electrode was incubated at room temperature for 1 h, washed with ultrapure water and blow-dried with N2 in sequence to obtain MCH/ T-DNA/Ti3C2TX-CT-Au/GCE.

The H1 solution (10 μL, 1 μM) was mixed with 10 μL of fipronil standard solution or sample solution and incubated at 37 ℃ for 30 min to form H1/fipronil composite. Its 10 μL was mixed with 10 μL H2 solution (0.5 μM) were dropped onto the surface of as-prepared modified electrode. Followed by incubation at room temperature for 90 min and washing with the PBS of pH 7.4 for 3 times. The resulting modified electrode was subject to the DPV measurement in the PBS of pH 7.4.
Reagents, electrochemical measurement, HPLC-MS detection condition and sample pretreatment were listed in ESI.

Fig. S1 presents one scheme for synthesis of Ti3C2TX-CT-Au hydrogel. Firstly, HAuCl4 was reduced with ascorbic acid to produce trioctahedron gold nanocrystal (TO-Au) (shown in Fig. S2). Then, the structural evolution of TO-Au was carried out in the presence of L-glutathione. The resultant CT-Au was combined with Ti3C2TX to produce Ti3C2TX-CT-Au hydrogel by self-assembly of Ti3C2TX nanosheets in the presence of A-GQD+. By hydrogen bond, electrostatic attraction and π-π stacking, A-GQD+ can be firmly fixed at Ti3C2TX nanosheets. As a result, the introduction of A-GQD+ leads to the production of Ti3C2TX-CT-Au hydrogel interweaving of Ti3C2TX nanosheets. Further, Fig. 1 A and B show the microstructures in the hydrogel were interconnected to form one monolithic Ti3C2TX network. This helps to enhance the viscosity of Ti3C2TX-CT-Au hydrogel. Basing on the results in Fig. 1C, the viscosity of Ti3C2TX-CT-Au hydrogel was found to be 231.5 mPa s. The value is more than 21-fold that in the absence of A-GQD+, verifying that the introduction of A-GQD+ can increase the viscosity.

The as-prepared Ti3C2TX-CT-Au hydrogel was printed on paper to fabricate an adhesive-free aptasensor. The structure and mechanical property of formed Ti3C2TX-CT-Au film were investigated by AFM and stress measurement. Fig. 1D and E show the Ti3C2TX-CT-Au film offers one three-dimensional structure. Different from Ti3C2TX film, many concave and convex microstructures appear on the Ti3C2TX-CT-Au film. This is attributed to strong interaction between the Ti3C2TX nanosheets and the CT-Au. The interaction strengthens the connection between different Ti3C2TX nanosheets and brings an improved mechanical property. Fig. 1F indicates the maximum stress of Ti3C2TX-CT-Au film is up to 26.4 MPa, which is more than 1.5-fold that of Ti3C2TX film. Such a good mechanical property helps to improve the repeatability of continuous detection.

Fig. 2 presents the SEM image of CT-Au and SEM, TEM, HRTEM images and element mappings of C, Ti, Au, S and Cl of Ti3C2TX-CT-Au film. It can be seen from Fig. 2A that CT-Au offers one Chinese tower-like nanostructure with the rich of edges and angles. The special structure is mainly due to the use of L-glutathione. L-glutathione is a chiral small molecule with sulfydryl group, it can induce a structural evolution process of gold nanocrystals by its stereoselective interaction with gold nanocrystal. This helps to produce the edges and angles on the surface of gold nanocrystal, leading to a more exposure of high-index faces. The TEM image shows the CT-Au nanocrystals were well dispersed on the Ti3C2TX nanosheets. The HRTEM image has two spacings of 0.24 and 0.22 nm, which correspond to the (002) facet of Ti3C2TX and (111) faces of gold nanocrystal. The results of element mapping demonstrate the spatial distribution of C, Ti, Au, S and Cl elements in the Ti3C2TX-CT-Au.

Fig. 3 presents total XPS spectrum, high-resolution spectra of Au4f, C1s, N1s and Ti2p of Ti3C2TX-CT-Au and XRD patterns of Ti3C2TX-CT-Au, CT-Au and Au. The total XPS spectrum includes eight peaks at 34.02, 60.07, 84.05, 285.01, 402.03, 456.09, 530.02 and 685.08 eV, corresponding to Ti3p, Ti3s, Au4f, C1s, N1s, Ti2p, O1s and Ti2s. The XPS spectrum of Au4f includes four peaks at 83.68, 84.58, 87.64 and 87.88 eV. The two peaks at 83.68 and 87.64 eV could be assigned to Au4f7/2 and Au4f5/2 of Au0, verifying the presence of Au0 specie. The two peaks at 84.58 and 87.88 eV could be assigned to Au4f7/2 and Au4f5/2 of Au+, verifying the presence of Au+ specie. As Au+ specie has a very low content, the peak area of Au4f7/2 and Au4f5/2 of Au+ is much less than that of Au0. The XPS spectrum of C1s includes four peaks at 281.6, 284.7, 284.9 and 288.8 eV, which could be assigned to C-Ti-O, C-C, Ti-C and C-O bonds. The XPS spectrum of N1s includes three peaks at 396.4, 398.3 and 400.2 eV, which could be assigned to the graphite N, pyrrole N and pyridine N. The XPS spectrum of Ti2p includes six peaks at 454.6, 455.7, 458.1, 460.8, 461.3 and 462.8 eV, which could be assigned to Ti-C2p3/2, Ti3+/Ti2+2p3/2, Ti-O2p3/2, Ti-C2p1/2, Ti3+/Ti2+2p1/2 and Ti-O2p1/2. The XRD pattern of Ti3C2TX-CT-Au includes six main diffraction peaks at 6.26°, 38.59°, 44.71°, 61.09°, 77.94° and 81.99°. The diffraction peak at 6.26° could be assigned to the (002) face of Ti3C2TX. The diffraction peaks at 38.59°, 44.71°, 61.09°, 77.94° and 81.99° could be assigned to the (111), (200), (220), (311) and (222) faces of gold nanocrystal, respectively. To evaluate the exposure of high-index faces, another gold nanocrystal (Au) was prepared by the same procedure in the synthesis of CT-Au unless no addition of L-glutathione. Fig. 3F indicates the XRD pattern of Au only offers two strong diffraction peaks at 6.24° and 38.60°, which could be assigned to (111) and (200) faces of gold nanocrystal. Different form Au, the XRD pattern of CT-Au offers eight strong diffraction peaks at 38.21°, 44.32°, 61.07°, 64.59°, 77.46°, 81.66°, 110.84° and 115.34°, which could be assigned to (111), (200), (220), (311), (222), (400), (331) and (420) facets of gold nanocrystal. The above results demonstrate that the CT-Au offers a more exposure of high-index faces. The exposure of high-index faces results in an improved catalytic activity. To confirm the above suggest, the SERS behavior of Rhodamine-6G on Ti3C2TX-CT-Au and Ti3C2TX-Au substrates was studied. Fig. S3 indicates the SERS peaks on Ti3C2TX-CT-Au substrate has a much better intensity compared with that on Ti3C2TX-Au substrate. This confirms the Ti3C2TX-CT-Au substrate can offer a stronger surface plasmon resonance compared with Ti3C2TX-Au, leading to an improved catalytic activity.

The construction of modified electrode mainly includes three steps. The first step is to form Ti3C2TX-CT-Au film on the GCE by dropping Ti3C2TX-CT-Au hydrogel. The second step is to immobilize the T-DNA on CT-Au in the Ti3C2TX-CT-Au film by Au-S bond. After that, the residual active sites on the surface of CT-Au were blocked by adding MCH. The third step is to transfer ferrocene molecule (Fc) to Ti3C2TX-CT-Au film. In the presence of fipronil, fipronil binds with H1 to form H1/fipronil composite. Since H1 contains one base sequence of fipronil aptamer, it can specifically combine with fipronil to form stable H1/fipronil composite [23,24]. Meanwhile, the hairpin structure of T-DNA was opened and leads to full exposure of the base sequence that can hybridize with T-DNA. Thus, the formed H1/fipronil composite hybridizes with T-DNA on Ti3C2TX-CT-Au film to produce T-DNA/H1/fipronil composite. Next, H2 replaces T-DNA/H1/fipronil composite to produce H2/T-DNA composite. This leads to the release of one H1/fipronil composite. As H2 contains Fc probe, the formation of H2/T-DNA composite achieves to the fixation of Fc molecules at the surface of Ti3C2TX-CT-Au film. Their redox on the electrode surface produces sensitive electrochemical detection signal. Next, the released H1/fipronil binds with T-DNA to trigger the next DNA cycle. By the DNA cycle, one fipronil molecule can bring many Fc molecules to the surface of Ti3C2TX-CT-Au film. Their redox produces a significant signal amplification.

Ten reaction pathways of DNA cycle amplification were designed to verify the sensing principle by native PAGE analysis. Fig. 4 B shows the mixture (lane 5) of D-1, D-2, D-3 and D-4 gives one bright band (T-DNA) with a lower electrophoretic migration rate compared to individual D-1 (lane 1), D-2 (lane 2), D-3 (lane 3) and D-4 (lane 4). The result confirms that D-1, D-2, D-3 and D-4 can be changed into T-DNA by base hybridization. No bands of H1 and fipronil appear on the electropherogram of mixture of H1 and fipronil (lane 7). Its electropherogram only appears H1/fipronil band. This demonstrates all H1 and fipronil molecules have been changed into T-DNA. The electropherogram of mixture of H1/fipronil, H2 and T-DNA shows one bright band with a low electrophoretic migration rate (T-DNA/H2), one dark band with a higher electrophoretic migration rate (H1/fipronil), and one bright band with the brightest electrophoretic migration rate (H2). This confirms the H2 can replace H1/fipronil to form T-DNA/H2. However, the electropherogram of mixture of H1, H2 and T-DNA (lane 10) don’t appear the band of T-DNA/H2, verifying in the absence of fipronil no T-DNA/H2 can form.

The CV and EIS behaviors of different electrodes were investigated to further understand the above sensing principle. Fig. 4C and D shows the CV curves and Nyquist plots of different electrodes in 1.0 mM K4Fe(CN)6 in the PBS of pH 7.4. Basing on the equivalent circuit in insert in Fig. 4D, the impedance parameters of different electrodes were calculated and the results were shown in Table S1. From Fig. 4C and D and Table S1, the modification of Ti3C2TX-CT-Au achieves to a significant increase in CV current and a reduce in charge transfer resistance (Rct). This is attributed to the catalysis of Ti3C2TX-CT-Au towards oxidation and reduction of K4Fe(CN)6 on the electrode surface. Different from Ti3C2TX-CT-Au, the modification of T-DNA, MCH and H1/fipronil leads to an obvious reduce in CV current and an increase in Rct value. This is because MCH, T-DNA and H1/fipronil are non-conductive species. Their modification blocks the part of channels for the electron transfer during the electrode reaction. This causes a reduce in the CV current with the increase in the Rct value. However, the introduction of H2 will lead to an increase CV current in the presence of fipronil. This is because the replacement of fipronil in the H1/fipronil composite by H2. The H2 contains Fc probe, thus the above replacement achieves to the fixation of Fc probe on the modified electrode surface. Their redox produces sensitive CV current response, indicating an increased CV current with the reduced Rct value. In addition, the catalysis of Ti3C2TX-CT-Au towards the redox of Fc probe further enhances the detection signal. As a result, the integration of Ti3C2TX-CT-Au with the DNA cycle achieves to a significant signal amplification. This will greatly improve the sensitivity for electrochemical detection of fipronil. In addition to the Rct value, Table S1 also shows the solution resistance (Rs), double-layer capacitance (Ci) and Warburg impedance (ZW). Similar to Rct, the introduction of Ti3C2TX-CT-Au, T-DNA, MCH, H1/fipronil composite and H2 brings a change in the Rs, Ci and Zw values. The result demonstrates that any change in the modification layer during the modified electrode preparation can cause sensitive electrochemical responses of the three-electrode testing system. In fact, every change in the electrochemical properties can serve as a signal for detection of fipronil.

The CV curves at different scan rates of proposed modified electrode in 1 mM K4Fe(CN)6 solution were measured to evaluate the reaction kinetics behavior. It can be seen from Fig. 4E that the increase of scan rate causes a rapid increase in CV current. However, the peak potential change is very small. This demonstrates the modified layer on the GCE can offer good electron/ion conductivity. Further, Fig. S4 indicates the CV peak current gives a good linear relationship with the square root of scanning rate, verifying the electrode reaction process mainly depends on the electrolyte diffusion.

To examine the electrochemical response towards fipronil, the DPV curves of proposed modified electrode in the PBS of pH 7.4 were measured in the absence and the presence of 1 × 10-13 M fipronil. Fig. 5 A shows that in the absence of fipronil no obvious peak appears on the DPV curve. However, the presence of fipronil appears an obvious peak current increase in the DPV curve, indicating a sensitive electrochemical response towards fipronil. This is because the presence of fipronil leads to a firm trigger of catalytic hairpin assembly. By the DNA cycle, one fipronil molecule can transfer many Fc molecules to the chip surface and leads to an obviously increased DPV current by the redox of these Fc probes under the catalysis of Ti3C2TX-CT-Au.

The effect of T-DNA concentration, T-DNA incubation time, H2 incubation time and H2 concentration were examined to optimize the detection conditions. Based on the results in Fig. 5B and C and Fig. S5, the optimized conditions, including T-DNA concentration of 0.5 μM, T-DNA incubation time of 60 min, H2 incubation time of 90 min and H2 concentration of 0.5 μM, were selected for the electrochemical detection of fipronil.

The influence of inorganic ions, organic small molecules, biological macromolecule and pesticides that may exist in vegetable and fruit on detection of 1 × 10−13 M fipronil were studied to evaluate the selectivity of proposed method. As inorganic ions mostly are electrochemical inertness under the detection conditions, the presence of 1.0 mg of Ca2+, Mg2+, Na+, K+, Fe3+, Cu2+, Zn2+, CO3 2−, PO4 3-, SO4 2−, Cl− and I- brings a DPV peak current change of less than 2.2 %. Although some organic small molecules and biological macromolecules may be oxidized and reduced, producing one strong electrochemical signal. However, these have been removed from the electrode surface during the electrode washing step. Thus, 1.0 mg of glucose, ascorbic acid, citric acid, BSA and starch only cause a peak current change of less than 2.4 %. The pesticides in vegetable and fruit are most likely to interfere with detection of fipronil because of their similar structure and properties. Fig. 5D shows only fipronil can produce a sensitive peak current response among carbendazim (Car), thiabendazole (Thi), aldicarb (Ald), phoxim (Pho), isocarbophos (Iso), chlorpyrifos (Chl) and fipronil. Due to the specific recognition of fipronil by the aptamer in H1, only fipronil can trigger the DNA cycle among the pesticides used. Because of the lack of Fc probes on the chip surface, the other pesticides cannot cause an obvious change in the DPV current. The above results demonstrate that the as-proposed method offers a high selectivity for electrochemical detection of fipronil.

Under the optimized conditions, the DPV curves in the presence of different concentration of fipronil were measured. Fig. 5E shows the increase of fipronil concentration brings an increased DPV current. This is because a high concentration of fipronil can produce more H1/fipronil composites during the incubation. This accelerates the DNA cycle and result more Fc probes were brought to the surface of modified electrode. Their redox produces a higher DPV current. Further, the relationship curve of DPV peak current with the logarithmic value of fipronil concentration was shown in Fig. 5F. The DPV peak current (Ip) will linearly increase with increasing the fipronil concentration in the range of 1 × 10−18 −1 × 10−13 M. There is one good linear relationship between Ip value and logarithmic value (LOG (Cfipronil, M) of the fipronil concentration. The corresponding regression equation is Ip (nA) = 121.55 × LOG(Cfipronil, M) + 2263.6, affording a correlation coefficient (R2) of 0.995 and detection limit of 4.3 × 10−19 M (S/N = 3). The sensitivity is better than that of all electrochemical sensors in Table 1 .

Ten modified electrode were prepared and used for the DPV detection of 1 × 10−13 M fipronil by the same procedure. The relative standard deviation (RSD) for the ten modified electrode is 1.2 %, indicating a high reproducibility (shown in Fig. S6). One modified electrode was used for 30 consecutive DPV detection of 1 × 10−13 M fipronil. The consecutive detection is the RSD of 1.8 %, indicating good repeatability (shown in Fig. S7). The modified electrode was stored at 4 °C for 30 days and then used for the DPV detection of 1 × 10−13 M fipronil. The RSD of 3.2 % was obtained for one 30-day storage, indicating a good storage stability (shown in Fig. S8).

The vegetable and fruit samples were purchased and pre-treated by one reported method (see 1.4 section in ESI). The collected supernatants were used for the fipronil detection by the as-proposed method and HPLC-MS. Table 2 shows the spiked recoveries of fipronil are between 97.6 % and 102.4 %. The results obtained by proposed method are consistent with that obtained by HPLC-MS. These confirm the as-proposed method has a high reliability for detection of fipronil.

The study demonstrates one solution for the construction of Ti3C2TX-nanogold nanocomposite. Introduction of L-glutathione induces one structural evolution of gold nanocrystal and improves the exposure of high-index facets. Introduction of protonated graphene quantum dot caused a rapid self-assembly of Ti3C2TX nanosheets to form highly viscous Ti3C2TX-nanogold hydrogel. The Ti3C2TX-nanogold offers excellent catalytic activity, conductivity and mechanical property. The adhesive-free modified electrode based on Ti3C2TX-nanogold exhibits a better sensitivity, selectivity and reproducibility compared with other sensors for detection of fipronil coupled with DNA cycle amplification. The study can also be used for construction of Ti3C2TX-based nanocomposites in biosensor, catalysis and energy storage.

Li Ruiyi: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Validation, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Wei Mengyu: . Sun Xiulan: . Li Zaijun: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Investigation, Validation, Visualization, Writing – review & editing. Liu Xiaohao: .
